subject: [zzzzteana] Man admits Soham kidnapping hoax calls
body:
A man named Howard Youde has admitted to making hoax calls to police investigating the disappearance of Soham schoolgirls Jessica Chapman and Holly Wells.  He pleaded guilty to one count of wasting police time on August 15th, 2002, at Wrexham Magistrates Court in North Wales.

Youde, a 45-year-old from Hope, near Wrexham, made three calls to Cambridgeshire police claiming to have abducted the young girls. He was arrested in Wrexham in the early hours of August 16th after officers traced the calls to a phone box on Brook Street.

His defense lawyer, Mark Arden, stated that the offense was neither premeditated nor calculated, but acknowledged the severity of Youde's actions. Arden emphasized the distress caused to the families and called Youde's actions "horrific" and "unforgivable."

Despite claiming to have no recollection of making the calls due to heavy drinking, Youde has been released on unconditional bail until November 7th, when he will be sentenced. The court has adjourned the hearing for pre-sentence reports, warning Youde that custody is a possibility.

subject: Re: [Python-3000] lambda
body:
Lennart Regebro suggested that "anonymous function" would be a more accurate name for lambda, and that using "def anonymously" with normal return syntax would make it more readable. 

He provided an example:

```python
def(a,b): return a+b
```

instead of

```python
lambda a, b: a+b
```

Nick responded humorously, acknowledging that the current syntax's ugliness might be a point in its favor. He emphasized that giving a function a name generally improves readability and self-documentation.

subject: PAPER 6
body:
Beehive is a system designed to enhance the performance of structured DHT peer-to-peer networks like Pastry.  Motivated by the demanding performance, scalability, and adaptivity requirements of applications like DNS and web servers, which often exhibit power-law query distributions, Beehive focuses on improving average case lookup performance to O(1). 

This is achieved through dynamic popularity estimation and proactive data replication. Beehive utilizes a fractional parameter, C, which adjusts in real-time to ensure that C fraction of queries are satisfied at the source node (knowing the answer before asking). If not, queries will only involve an average of O(1) nodes, with a worst-case scenario of O(log n).

Unlike passive replication, where copies are stored along the query path, Beehive employs proactive replication by assigning a replication level (i) to each object. Objects at level i are replicated on nodes with at least i matching prefixes. This ranges from level 0, where an object is stored on every node, to log_b n, where an object is only stored on its home node. Beehive aims to find the minimal replication level for each object to maintain an average lookup time of C. The paper details the optimization calculations and provides a closed-form

subject: california update 6 / 08 / 01
body:
The CPUC has delayed the vote on paying QFs 15% of their undercollected debt. Sources believe this delay stems from two primary concerns. 

Firstly, issuing what could be perceived as a "preference payment" to the QFs amidst concerns about potential bankruptcy issues raises red flags. The CPUC, operating within the current economic climate, must carefully consider such implications. Secondly, there's a lack of clarity regarding the implementation of the 15% payment. 

This delay, while unsurprising, is typical of the CPUC's operational style. It's also worth noting that both SoCal and PG&E currently face numerous claims on their cash flows.  Furthermore, agreeing to pay 15% of debts to the QFs presents legal challenges.  Utilities paying some creditors but not others could increase the likelihood of an involuntary bankruptcy filing against SoCal by non-QF creditors.

subject: Re: [ILUG] extending a HAN with Wireless..
body:
Hello Justin,

To connect your new PC and notebook wirelessly, the simplest setup would be to use a wireless access point. Connect this access point to your existing 5-port hub.  

For your notebook, you can use a wireless PCMCIA network card. For your desktop machines, consider using PCMCIA to PCI adapters or wireless PCI network cards.

The access point will need to be configured with a network name (SSID) and a channel number for your wireless network.  You'll then configure your client machines (both the notebook and desktop) with the same network name. They should automatically detect the correct channel number.

Driver support for wireless network cards is generally good. There are only a few common chipsets, and Linux drivers are available for most, except for the very latest 802.11b Plus (22Mbps) and 802.11a hardware.

If you need further assistance or information, please check out the forums and IRC channel at [link].

Best regards,

Robert Fitzsimons
robfitz@273k.net

subject: Kevin Mitnick's laptop, signed by Woz, on eBay
body:
On eBay, you can find a piece of tech history: Kevin Mitnick's infamous Toshiba Satellite T1960CS laptop, signed by both Kevin Mitnick and Steve "Woz" Wozniak. 

This 486 laptop was seized by the FBI in 1995 during Mitnick's arrest, marking a significant moment in the history of computer hacking.  The laptop is fully functional and comes with a fresh installation of Windows 95, along with a shrink-wrapped copy on floppy disk.  It includes the original AT&T 14.4 PCMCIA modem card, power adapter, documentation, and even the FBI evidence bags.

The laptop's most unique feature is the signature of Steve Wozniak, Apple co-founder, on the bottom.  His message reads, "You've got the whole world in your hands. --Woz (Free Kevin!)."  Mitnick himself also signed the laptop.  

This laptop was featured on an episode of The Screen Savers on TechTV in 2002, where Wozniak and Mitnick authenticated the signatures. A tape of that episode is included with the sale.

Adam,

You mentioned that product bundling works, which is an interesting point.

You also said that this arrangement is similar to "normal" marriage, except the woman gets a man in a country with opportunities for herself and her children, and avoids certain negative choices.  

Let me be clear: we don't live in a fantasy world where women are solely defined by their domestic roles. Women are capable of being productive, independent individuals who are not limited to cooking, cleaning, or serving as sexual objects.  

Sometimes, and this might surprise you, men and women actually fall in love and choose to marry for companionship and mutual affection.  It's a concept that might seem idealistic, but it's a reality for many people.  

If a man approached my mother 25 years ago and tried to convince her to marry him based solely on her cleaning skills, we wouldn't be having this conversation.

These situations can work if both parties are aware that it's a transactional arrangement: he wants a maid, a companion, and a sexual partner without financial obligation, while she seeks a country with economic stability and a lower risk of AIDS.  It's a contract, not a marriage based on love.

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] thoughts on having EOFError inherit from	EnvironmentError?
body:
Guido van Rossum wrote:

> I don't think of EOFError as an environmental error... This is quite
> a different level of error than what EnvironmentError typically means

Greg Ewing responded:

> I think it depends. Any "expected" EOF Errors are going to be
> caught by the surrounding code before propagating very far.
> An *uncaught* EOFError probably means that a file was shorter
> than you expected it to be, which counts as an environmental
> error to my way of thinking.

No, that's more of a parsing error. EnvironmentError doesn't deal with the contents of files.

Greg continued:

> My current coding style involves wrapping an "except EnvironmentError"
> around any major operation and reporting it as a "File could not be
> read/written/whatever because..." kind of message. Having
> EOFError get missed by that would be a nuisance.

But what operations actually raise EOFError? Are you using raw_input()? It's primarily used for educational purposes. 



-- 
--Guido van Rossum (home page: http://www.python.org/~guido/)
_______________________________________________
Python-Dev mailing list

subject: RE: Hanson's Sept 11 message in the National Review
body:
Bill,

You raise an interesting question: how effective is rudeness and disrespect in changing someone's mind? Is this truly the path to winning friends and influencing people?

Firstly, I doubt the intention is to sway Rumsy's opinion. Instead, it's likely a strategy to demonstrate a vocal and forceful opposition to his views. By making such a strong and public stand, they hope to garner more attention and coverage for their dissenting ideas, effectively spreading their message.  Think of it like a virus; it doesn't necessarily need the host's cooperation, but rather forces the host to either develop defenses or adapt.

Historically, the "in your face" approach has been used to gain popular support and fuel grassroots movements. Consider the bold, confrontational signature of Hancock on the Declaration of Independence, or the Chicago Seven trial. These are just a few examples of how this tactic has been employed to challenge authority and galvanize support.

Now, I understand that this approach can be annoying. And if you find yourself annoyed, then the "in your facers" have achieved their goal.  It's a tactic designed to provoke a reaction, and those who are easily agitated become fuel for the fire.

Personally, I find the politics

subject: Re: Forged whitelist spam
body:
Subject: Re: Forged whitelist spam

On Sun, 11 Aug 2002, Russell Turpin wrote:

> If you're using the mail client on your personal machine, there's no reason you would need to enter a passphrase, unless that is part of how you secure the data on your personal machine. You're private key is as secure as any other data on your machine. If you're working remotely, you already have to enter a passphrase to get to your email.

The original comment was about digital signatures. A digital signature is essentially useless if any user-level application or malicious code can access your keyring in plain text and send messages on your behalf.  A passphrase used to unlock the keyring for a specific use case offers minimal protection (as it's not immune to passphrase-snatching tools), but it's significantly better than leaving your key constantly accessible.

subject: Re: [opensuse] openSUSE 11.0 and the Non-ready KDE4
body:
Aaron Kulkis and James R Thompson discussed the idea of making openSUSE more user-friendly, with James suggesting that the focus should be on making it accessible to everyone, not just tech-savvy users.

Aaron countered that while usability is important, it shouldn't come at the expense of configurability. He emphasized that every user has unique needs and that reducing customization options would ultimately harm both new and experienced users.

M9 agrees with Aaron, stating that achieving the desired outcome requires a two-pronged approach:

1. **Usability:** Applications should function flawlessly, be easy to use, and work seamlessly together.
2. **Configurability:** The desktop environment should be highly customizable to cater to the diverse needs of all users.

M9 concludes by suggesting that collaborative efforts to identify and resolve bugs will accelerate the development of a system that meets everyone's requirements.

subject: Fw: The Gold Buddha
body:
The story of the Gold Buddha is a powerful metaphor for the hidden potential within each of us. In 1957, a group of Thai monks were relocating a giant clay Buddha when one of them discovered a crack in the statue. Concerned about damaging the idol, they decided to wait a day before continuing. That night, a monk shone his flashlight on the Buddha and noticed something reflecting back from the crack. Driven by curiosity, he used a hammer and chisel to chip away at the clay.  As he worked, the Buddha grew brighter and brighter, eventually revealing a magnificent solid-gold statue.

Historians believe the Buddha had been covered in clay centuries earlier to protect it from Burmese invaders.  The monks, hoping to save the treasure, hid it under layers of clay.  Tragically, all the monks were killed during the attack, leaving the gold Buddha hidden for centuries until its rediscovery in 1957.

Just like the Buddha, our outer shell often masks our true potential. We, too, unconsciously hide our inner gold beneath layers of self-doubt, limiting beliefs, and fear.  To uncover our own "gold," we must have the courage to chip away at our outer shell, piece by piece.

subject: Help Needed -Reply
body:
You don't need to program the code into the eeprom.  Simply place it inside a `main()` function block like this:

```c
void main(void)
{
  YOUR CODE
}
```

Remember to include the start button logic at the beginning of your code.  The handyboard will automatically execute the `main()` function when the start button is pressed. The battery will retain the code in RAM even when the board is switched "off". 


You mentioned wanting to run your C program on the handyboard without a connection to your PC and use the start button to initiate execution. To achieve this, you don't need to convert your C file to an S19 file for eeprom programming.

subject: Re: [UAI] Imprecise Probabilities--A simple and yet computationally nontrivial problem
body:
Lotfi Zadeh posed a question regarding the marginal probability distribution of a random variable X, given a joint probability matrix P with entries represented as "approximately aij."  These entries, aij, are within the unit interval and sum to unity. Zadeh presented four specific interpretations for "approximately aij," ranging from intervals to triangular fuzzy numbers and probability density functions.

Adopting a Bayesian perspective, I view each entry P_ij (representing the probability of X=i and Y=j) as a random variable itself.  The matrix P is defined through an approximate (imprecise) matrix A, which is specified in a Bayesian context using prior information, likelihood functions, and data. In contrast, within a fuzzy context, A is provided directly.

It's crucial to recognize that the variables P_ij are not independent. The sum of all P_ij entries must equal 1, necessitating the rejection of any combinations consistent with A but resulting in a non-normalized P.  A's structure should ensure the existence of at least one valid combination.

Instead of addressing Zadeh's specific cases (a-d), I will assume that the n*n matrix P is a normalized random vector.  Furthermore, I

subject: RE: [ILUG] SUSE 8 disks? (thread changed slightly)
body:
Rick Moen suggested that if we don't like SuSE's product licensing, we could create our own distribution, even using components from SuSE while adhering to their licensing terms.  He posed the question: how difficult would this actually be?

It's true that there aren't any Linux kernel hackers or distributions originating from Ireland, as far as I know.  Building a distribution, something like "Dolmen Linux," would undoubtedly be challenging, especially when it comes to the packaging system.  There are differing opinions on the best approach, with some favoring a FreeBSD-style ports system, others Debian-style, and still others sticking with RPM.

While other LUGs have done interesting things, like creating a Blackbox version, the idea of building a full-fledged distribution is daunting. It might be fun, but it's also a huge undertaking.

I've been pondering the same idea for a couple of months, but always thought it too complicated.  It's not that I dismiss other projects, like Niall's work on the B.B.C., but I wonder if there's a general interest in an Irish distribution. What do you all think?

subject: [ILUG] Optimising for PentiumMMX?
body:
I've been experimenting with an old Dell OptiPlex5110 and decided to try building a Linux system optimized for the PentiumMMX processor.

To begin, I installed a minimal Debian system for compiling programs.  I then built a custom kernel, as most CPU optimizations are best implemented at the kernel level.  Next, I built X4.2.0 from source code, as X heavily utilizes matrices and can benefit from MMX optimizations.  Following the initial build, I was encouraged to explore the CVS versions of both XFree86 and the DRI components, so I plan to rebuild X once any bugs are resolved. Finally, I built KDE3.0BETA1 from source code, again to fully leverage the MMX processor.

Are there any other essential tools you recommend rebuilding for further performance improvements? I understand not all packages benefit from being built for PentiumMMX instead of the i386 architecture Debian typically uses, but have I covered all the potential candidates?

I'm curious about rebuilding C and other libraries. Could they utilize Pentium and MMX instructions to enhance system performance? Would this necessitate rebuilding gcc as well?

I realize not everything benefits from recompilation. For example, I doubt recompiling bash would yield

body:
The construction of railways, bridges, and new railway lines was a significant undertaking in the east, with some projects even being completed during the war.  The Empress Frederick played a crucial role in this endeavor, collaborating with Building Councilor Inne, whom she affectionately called "the modern Schlxitef," to develop plans and a detailed model that received unanimous approval.

On two occasions, Inne offered his resignation, which I declined, seeking to offer him comfort instead.  In the first half of 1912, Sir Ernest Cassel arrived with a verbal note from England. This note proposed neutrality for England in the event of an unprovoked attack on Germany, contingent upon Germany limiting its naval construction program and abandoning its new Naval bill.

The note, which Cassel presented to me, was a formal offer of neutrality in future conflicts, but it came with conditions: limitations on Germany's naval construction program, to be discussed and agreed upon through mutual conferences.

The decision was made to have Grey, due to his personal and official importance, appear only at the conclusion of the negotiations to sign the agreement.  He was instructed to join the festivities and fireworks, a German expression for enjoying the Bengal light illumination.

subject: Re: Canadians
body:
Canada's reliance on franchises like Wendy's, KFC, Pizza Hut, Taco Bell, and Tim Hortons is a stark contrast to the USA's entrepreneurial spirit.  While Gary Lawrence Murphy suggests Canada is built on monopolies, things aren't entirely bleak. I recall Vancouver boasting a diverse selection of excellent local restaurants, and Toronto offering a variety of local establishments, though perhaps of a different kind.  

I've never indulged in a Canadian doughnut, so I can't comment on its quality.  Both Vancouver and Toronto are cities I could comfortably live in, but Carolyn prefers warmer climates.

Personally, I rarely dine at franchise restaurants.  Local eateries often provide superior food at a better price, or both!

subject: The GOv gets tough on Net Users.....er Pirates..
body:
The Department of Justice (DOJ) is signaling a potential shift from talk to action in its fight against online copyright infringement. Deputy Assistant Attorney General John Malcolm believes criminal prosecutions are necessary to protect the American content industry, arguing that individuals who share copyrighted material often believe they are acting legally. 

RIAA President Cary Sherman, who was on the same panel as Malcolm, echoed this sentiment, stating that criminal prosecutions could deter file-sharing. He believes that the threat of legal repercussions would make individuals think twice before engaging in such activities.

However, the effectiveness of this approach is questionable.  Previous civil suits and warnings issued to individuals have had a minimal impact on the file-sharing community. Criminal lawsuits, with the potential for prison sentences, could further alienate groups like the RIAA and face challenges due to the widespread nature of file-sharing. Targeting individual servers might be a more feasible approach.

The DOJ's stance is supported by the No Electronic Theft (NET) Act, which defines copyright infringement and outlines potential penalties. The Act defines "financial gain" broadly, encompassing the receipt of any value, including other copyrighted works, even if no money is exchanged. Determining the value of a copyrighted work, however, remains unclear, as it can vary significantly depending on the

subject: Re: [Baseline] Raising chickens the high-tech way
body:
I still disagree with the idea that artificial selection is genetic engineering.  While it does utilize the principles of genetics, it's not the same as intentionally manipulating genes in a controlled lab setting.

My reasoning is this: if we're going to be so loose with the definition of genetic engineering, then wouldn't that mean I, a naturally born human, am also a genetically engineered organism? My parents, after all, chose each other based on their physical traits and hoped for certain genetic combinations in their offspring.  They certainly influenced the process, but they didn't directly engineer the genes themselves.

Perhaps the reason for my disagreement stems from the fact that some groups opposing regulations on genetic engineering use a similar argument: "We've been doing this since prehistory, so why worry about caution now?"

body:
Dave Long wrote on Thu, 2002-09-12 at 12:22:

> ... randomising letters in the middle of words has little or no effect on the ability of skilled readers to understand the text. This is easy to demonstrate. In a publication of New Scientist, you could randomize all the letters, keeping the first two and last two the same, and readability would hardly be affected. My analysis didn't come to much because the theory at the time focused on shape and sequence recognition. Saberi's work suggests we may have some powerful parallel processors at work. The reason for this is surely that identifying content by parallel processing speeds up recognition. We only need the first and last two letters to spot changes in meaning.

I'm working with an experimental text recognition/processing engine that exhibits similar characteristics. It can read right through misspellings like the one above without any difficulty. As the author mentioned, the pattern matching is inherently parallel internally. 

If the text recognition algorithm/architecture humans use is similar to the one we've been working with, the importance of the first letter (and to a lesser extent, the last letter) is that without it, the text pattern recognition problem becomes

subject: Re: [SAdev] Spam added to regular email
body:
Marc,

You mentioned you're adding low-scoring spam messages (5-18 points) to your spam corpus by hand-sorting emails.  You've noticed some of these messages are newsletters or other legitimate emails containing spam advertisements.

You're questioning how to classify these because catching spam within a newsletter or other accepted email isn't a false positive.

I don't see the issue.  Either the entire message is unwanted spam that you didn't consent to receive, or it's something you agreed to, like a mailing list. If you agreed to receive it, it's not spam and shouldn't be flagged, regardless of any advertisements it may contain. 


Tony

subject: Re: Battery Level Indicator
body:
You're likely right, you may have damaged your analog 5 input. Analog inputs are very susceptible to overvoltage. If you applied more than +5V directly to the input, it's possible you damaged it.

When I first received my HC11EVB, I mistakenly connected 12V directly to the E port (one of the pins) and was certain I had ruined the chip. I contacted Motorola's tech support, and they assured me that 12V on an A/D port shouldn't be an issue.  Thankfully, everything still functioned correctly.

I believe the "pink book" mentions that 200% of the reference voltage is acceptable.

subject: real
body:
While studying for my PhD qualifying exam, I encountered a problem I'm struggling to solve. I was hoping you might be able to take a look.

Let {f_n} be a sequence of measurable functions defined on a measurable set E with finite measure.  Assume that the family {f_n} is pointwise bounded, meaning for each x ∈ E, there exists a constant M_x such that |f_n(x)| ≤ M_x for all n. 

My question is: can you show that for any epsilon > 0, there exists a closed set F ⊂ E with m(E - F) < epsilon, such that the family is uniformly bounded on F? In other words, can you demonstrate the existence of a constant M such that |f_n(x)| ≤ M for all x ∈ F and all n?

Any assistance you could provide would be greatly appreciated. Thank you for your time,

Sarah Wright

body:
Subject: [DMDX] Re: question about randomization

Hi all,

I'm having some trouble with randomization in my experiment using an implicit priming paradigm. I'm hoping someone can help me out.

My experiment has four conditions, and I want them to appear in a fixed order. Within each condition, there are six blocks, and I want the order of these blocks to be randomized. Each block contains a bitmap file followed by three randomized items.  I'd like to have a scramble emit command, represented by a backslash, between each block at the start of a line. For example:

```
\\
```

How can I achieve this? Where should I place the "$" symbols in my script?

You'll need to use the multi-scrambling options for this complex setup. There's a helpful example in the DMDX documentation that's very similar to what you're looking for: http://psy1.psych.arizona.edu/~jforster/dmdx/help/dmdxhmultiscramblingscramblekeyword.htm.  You'll need to replace the backslash with a  but otherwise, it's a direct adaptation of the first example.

subject: Backtracking in Icon
body:
Subject: Backtracking in Icon

I was intrigued by the recent "Icon Analyst" article discussing the frequency of control structure usage in Icon programs. While it briefly mentioned backtracking, it didn't delve into its practical applications.

Initially, when I discovered backtracking and goal-seeking behavior in Icon, I was excited. It seemed like a powerful tool, allowing me to achieve backtracking capabilities similar to Prolog without resorting to complex loops and if statements.

However, despite having this robust backtracking mechanism at my disposal, I find myself using it sparingly.  Most of my applications involve simple constructs like:

    (checkValid(x) | write("error"))

which, while technically utilizing backtracking, feel more like a stylistic choice to emulate a "cool" Icon programmer rather than a genuinely necessary technique.

I'm curious to know if others utilize backtracking more extensively in their Icon programs.  Is it a feature that proves more valuable in certain contexts, or is it primarily a "neat" but underutilized aspect of the language?

Could the absence of logical variables, a feature present in Prolog, be a contributing factor to the limited use of backtracking and goal-seeking evaluation in Icon?


-- 
----------------------
Dave Ku

subject: Re: [ILUG] SUSE 8 disks? (thread changed slightly)
body:
Paul Linehan wrote:

> You're further confused if you think the GNU GPL gives you the right to get covered software for free. It does not.

> All I want is the OS and GNU apps. I was under the impression that for those, all one needed was to pay for the media and not for the code itself? Am I wrong?

The GPL states that if you legally obtain a copy of the binary version, you have the right to receive the corresponding source code.

My understanding was that binaries or any other format could be obtained for the cost of the media plus a token of appreciation, which would be subject to agreement between the parties. I was offering a bottle of wine, maybe a pint or two, and a chat for a copy – I'm not sure what the going rate is.

The GPL doesn't specify anything about charging for binaries. You could, for example, download the sources for GNU's /bin/true, compile it yourself, and try selling the resulting binary for EUR 1000. (And, with a good enough sales pitch, you might even succeed!) However, the GPL does stipulate that you must provide the sources for that binary if asked.

subject: RE: The absurdities of life.
body:
You're right, it's absurd that companies like Pac Bell would go through the trouble and expense of sending a check for such a small amount.  It's likely they spent more on postage and materials than the $0.33 refund itself.  

This situation highlights a frustrating dilemma:  Should companies be required to issue refunds for even minuscule amounts, even if it's more costly for them?  John Hall mentioned a similar situation with an insurance company, where he received a check for a few cents.  

Perhaps the only alternatives are to create more complex systems with even greater time and labor costs, or simply keep the money.  

This issue reminds me of our own situation with AT&T cable in Jacksonville.  We're considering switching providers due to poor customer service and overcharging.  If the overage amount per customer is significant (around $30 or more), we'll likely issue direct refunds.  However, for smaller amounts, we'd probably combine the total and settle with the city as a lump sum.

In your case, maybe you could poll your customers and let them decide where the lump sum should go. Of course, they'd need to be notified by mail first.

subject: Digital input cct
body:
Hi there,

I'm working on building a handyboard and I've run into some trouble with part 7.2 (Digital Input cct).  The testdigitals(); function in hbtest.c isn't responding to the start button.

I've double-checked that all the chips and RP1 are installed correctly, and none of them are getting hot.  Interestingly, when I test pin1 of U5, the yellow Charge LED on the I/C board lights up fully. Is this normal behavior, or could it be a problem?

Even when testdigitals(); isn't running, the LED remains lit and it seems to disrupt communication between the board and the computer. This is for my final year project, and I've been stuck on this issue for quite some time.

Any help would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks,
Rob O' Neill

subject: 320p 4/9 Update: Soyuz docks with station
body:
A Russian Soyuz spacecraft, carrying two new crew members and a wealthy American space tourist, successfully docked with the International Space Station today, concluding a two-day orbital pursuit. The docking occurred at 3:10 p.m. EDT at the downward-facing port of the Russian Zarya module, as the two spacecraft traveled high above Eastern Europe.

On board Soyuz TMA-10 were spacecraft commander Oleg Kotov, Expedition 15 commander Fyodor Yurchikhin, and U.S. software developer Charles Simonyi, who paid over $20 million to become the fifth private citizen to visit the space station.  

"Contact and capture, docking confirmed, at 11:10 p.m. Moscow time (3:10 p.m. EDT)," announced NASA commentator Rob Navias from the Russian mission control center. "Docking confirmed, a perfect arrival. Soyuz TMA-10 has arrived at the International Space Station."

Martha Stewart, a close friend of Simonyi who witnessed the launch at the Baikonur Cosmodrome in Kazakhstan, was present at the Russian control center in Korolev for the docking.

subject: [Spambayes] Ditching WordInfo
body:
If you have enough memory available, using the pickle store might improve performance. It only needs to access the disk once, although it does require a significant disk write.  I haven't found any obvious ways to speed things up once the data is in memory. On my system, the current setup scores about 50 messages per second, starting from memory.

While this speed can be frustrating during my full test runs (one of which scores a message over 120,000 times and trains it more than 30,000 times), I'm not motivated to optimize it. For my personal email, the current speed wouldn't be noticeable. Guido might complain about waiting an extra second for his 50-message batches to score, but he tends to complain about everything.

Profiling would be necessary to identify any performance bottlenecks, and that's the kind of optimization I've decided against. I haven't profiled yet, but suspect there aren't any major issues.  The 5-gramming of long words with high-bit characters is likely expensive when it occurs, but it doesn't happen frequently.  Moreover, this approach isn't suitable for non-English languages, so speeding

body:
Subject: Re: some action around here

Hey Handy Board folks!

I've noticed our group has been pretty quiet lately. It's time to get things moving again!  Maybe some of you have exciting projects you'd like to share.

I'm currently working on a robot, which you can read about on the Seattle Robotics website at http://www.seattlerobotics.org/encoder/200001/docbot.html. While I didn't use a Handy Board for this project, I initially started with one but needed it for another project, so I switched to a different board.  It's worth noting that my current project could easily be accomplished with a Handy Board.

One interesting challenge I faced was creating a missing arcsine function for determining angles to walls.  Someone on this list previously contributed a series that I used, and it wasn't the traditional Taylor series. The Taylor series tends to lose accuracy near 90°, likely due to truncating terms after about 9, but this other function remained accurate even at 89° with the same number of terms in the polynomial expansion. I plan to publish the C code for both series as soon as I can identify the

subject: [9fans] 9fans@cse.psu.edu
body:
Russ Cox (rsc@pan9.bell-labs.com) suggested I try the following command:

A:\\> b hd!0!9dos

Here's the output I received:

Protected-mode bootstrap
hd0: LBA 8248472 sectors, -71237632 bytes
Cannot access device: fd!0!9dos
Boot devices: hd!0 h!0
boot from: 

It appears the system doesn't recognize my floppy controller. This suggests a potential incompatibility with the chipset or unexpected timing issues.

The manual lists the following chips on the board:

* Intel Pemtium-MMX
* 82437VX
* 82371SB

I'm unfamiliar with the Intel line and which chip handles the floppy drive, so I'll need to open the system to check the bus speeds.

I'll also experiment by moving the floppy controller to an AHA1540/42CP SCSI controller.

Thanks for your assistance!

subject: Re: [Razor-users] What's wrong with the Razor servers now?
body:
I'm curious about how Razor's trust system works in this situation.

I signed up for a free account on a website that provides access to a large dataset. As part of the signup process, I also subscribed to their mailing list. This list includes new articles, books, and links to their bookstore.

Despite agreeing to receive these emails and genuinely wanting to stay informed about new content, Razor consistently flags them as spam.  The emails clearly state how to unsubscribe, so they are not unsolicited commercial email.

Who is losing trust points in this scenario? If it's not spam, shouldn't Razor recognize that and avoid flagging it?

body:
Neal Norwitz listed the following platforms used for bug day testing:

* sparc solaris10
* amd64 gentoo (running Ubuntu 6.10)
* x86 gentoo (*)
* g4 os x (experiencing occasional SVN issues, despite being colocated with the SVN server)
* some win xp box (#4, considered the most stable recently)
* ia64 ubuntu
* ppc debian (possibly Ubuntu)
* ppc64 debian (possibly Ubuntu)

Jeroen Ruigrok van der Werven suggests adding at least one BSD platform to the mix. He notes that a successful BSD build typically translates well to other BSD systems. The current list leans heavily towards Linux (glibc) and might not effectively expose issues within that ecosystem, except for the commercial Solaris and OS X platforms.

subject: [DMDX]
body:
Hi,

I'm having trouble with bitmap presentations in DMDX version 1.1.13. The black and white images appear very low quality when run in DMDX.  They are saved as 24-bit files and look fine in Paintshop and in TimeDX version 1.05 when running a video mode test.  I've tried saving them with different color depths, but that hasn't helped.

Jonathan (j.c.f.) suggested that the issue might be due to a difference in screen resolution between TimeDX and DMDX.

I encountered a similar problem and solved it by specifying the video mode in the item file's parameter line using the keyword.  

For example, you would include something like this in the parameter line (insert your own values):

The values should match those used in the TimeDX video mode test (note there are five numbers). If  (or ) is not specified, DMDX defaults to 640x480 (480) 8 bpp 0Hz (thanks to j.c.f.'s help file and Mike Ford's tutorial). This aligns with j.c.f.'s point about screen resolution.

body:
Fred wrote:

I've had personal experience with Windows mail servers running NTFS, and they all required weekly defragmentation to maintain performance.  If you don't defragment them, performance suffers significantly. This wasn't the case with Linux and ext3 file systems.

James Knott previously stated that NTFS is fragmentation resistant, but I disagree. While it's true that NTFS is more resistant to fragmentation than FAT, it's not immune. Even NTFS partitions with ample free space can benefit from defragmentation.  

I encourage you to run the defragmenter on any NTFS partition that hasn't been defragged recently and observe the results.

subject: Re: [R] Rserve and R to R communication
body:
Paul,

Thanks for your message, but I'm not entirely clear on your point. Could you elaborate on your reasoning?

Are you suggesting that because two or more sub-processes initiated simultaneously might share the same default `set.seed` value for any RNG, this could be problematic? That seems unlikely. However, you're right that by mixing numbers from generators on different machines running in parallel, you're altering the pattern of the random numbers. This means the numbers generated wouldn't adhere to the established properties of the generator you're ostensibly using. Experts in random number generation are concerned about this type of issue.  You wouldn't be able to confidently claim you're using a specific scheme, which would be a significant problem if you intend to rely on the results or publish them.

My guess is that the numbers might be quite random, perhaps even difficult to reproduce (which is another concern). However, this is just a guess and not something you'd want to present as definitive. 



Let me know if you have any other thoughts. 

Matt



---

subject: Re: Microsoft buys XDegress - more of a p2p/distributed data thing...
body:
Mr. FoRK describes a file caching system in XDegrees where files are distributed across multiple internet-connected systems, similar to Napster or Freenet.  He highlights that XDegrees' caching is more advanced, allowing users with high bandwidth to download portions ("stripes") of a file concurrently from various cached locations. The software then reconstructs the complete file and utilizes digital signatures to ensure its integrity by comparing it to the original.  A key element of this signature process is a file digest, stored as an HTTP header.

This "more sophisticated" claim, however, appears to mirror the functionality implemented in numerous other P2P Content Delivery Networks (CDNs), including:

* Kazaa
* EDonkey/Overnet
* BitTorrent
* Gnutella (with significant extensions)
* OnionNetworks WebRAID

It's worth noting that the quality of the "digest" employed by each system can differ considerably.

- Gordon

subject: [Spambayes] understanding high false negative rate
body:
I had a similar experience with a high false negative rate. I experimented with a basic tokenizer for headers, splitting them into words and formatting them as "%s: %s" % (hdr, word).  Surprisingly, this worked too well! The "received" and "date" headers led the classifier to realize that most of my spam was old and most of my legitimate emails were new.

Initially, when I first started using this software, I encountered the opposite problem. I had collected spam for a week or two and then mixed it with randomly selected emails from my inbox.  The classifier immediately flagged "received:2001" as non-ham.  It's a good reminder to be mindful of the data source when collecting spam and ham examples.  It's probably worth mentioning this in the software documentation.

First, I removed the service manager from the extensions:

```python
win32_extensions += [
    WinExt_win32('servicemanager',
           extra_compile_args = ['-DUNICODE', '-D_UNICODE',

           libraries = "user32 ole32 advapi32 shell32",
           dsp_file = r"win32\\Pythonservice servicemanager.dsp",
           windows_h_version = 0x501), ############ change to 0x501
]

subject: Re: [9fans] FreeBSD 4.0 / Plan9 3rd ed.
body:
Russ,

You mentioned that the Linux drawterm won't work in most other places, even under binary emulation, because it relies on the task segment selector as a unique thread ID.  

I'm confused. "Other places" includes 2.4, for example. Why use architecture-dependent tricks when PID is readily available? On Linux, thread and process are synonymous.  Linux has a `clone(2)` equivalent to `rfork()`, and the model mirrors Plan 9's: a process is a collection of components that can be shared across processes; `fork()` is a specialized case of a more general operation that copies a subset of components and reuses the old ones for the rest.

While TSS worked on x86 before 2.3.11, it depended on the specifics of `task_struct` allocation. 2.4 uses TSS-per-CPU, eliminating the need to change registers; it's more efficient and increases the process limit while reducing TLB flushes.

Using `getpid()` is a more portable and sensible approach compared to `%tss`.

This same issue applies to Inferno Linux binaries, which also fail to work under other operating systems with Linux binary emulation.

subject: Re: Recommended Viewing
body:
The idea that we don't use all parts of our brain is a myth. We actually use it constantly, but our cultural conditioning creates a focal point that leads most of us to ignore and underutilize 99.999% of its potential. Think of it like radio stations: "Lucid" is a measure of the bandwidth of a notch filter. All stations are broadcasting, but we only choose to tune into Easy Rock 105.

For instance, your shoes are full of feet.  

When we lower these filters (using whatever method works for us), we see the full picture. It's not just that our shoes are full of toes, but those toes are brimming with bones, blood, muscle, and even toejam. All these elements are there, readily apparent, unless we keep our filters in place.

Life itself can feel like the ultimate spam.

-tom

subject: Re: Sorting dir output
body:
Subject: Re: Sorting dir output

Hello,

Craig Schneider asked:

> How could I execute a 'dir' command on a DOS system and store the output in an array, sort it by date, and move files older than 3 days into a folder named 'history'?

Here's a Perl solution:

```perl
# Open the current directory
opendir my $dh, '.' or die "Cannot open '.' $!";

# Get files older than three days
my @files = grep -M > 3, readdir $dh;

closedir $dh;

for my $file ( @files ) {
    rename $file, "history/$file" or die "Cannot move '$file' $!";
}
```

This code snippet opens the current directory, reads its contents into an array, filters for files older than three days, and then moves them into the 'history' folder.



John
-- 
Perl isn't a toolbox, but a small machine shop where you can special-order
certain sorts of tools at low cost and in short order.       -- Larry Wall

I recently acquired a TCOMP PortMaster board (by Ray's Tiny Racers) for use in a 'robot dispatch base station'. subject: [DMDX] Re: White background and grey text best combo?

You mentioned using a laptop with a large screen, which might be contributing to the issue.  Is black on white the standard for psychological experiments? It's become the common practice for computer displays.

As a first-time experimenter, I understand feeling "hypnotized" after a while.  Different colors can be tried, but the reasons behind color choices in displays relate to masked priming and display persistence.

My guess is that your display is 60Hz, a typical refresh rate for LCDs. The monitor's flickering from its rapidly decaying phosphors could be causing the problem.

subject: [DMDX] Re: DIY input device- RT's affected?
body:
Subject: [DMDX] Re: DIY input device- RT's affected?

Hi Andy,

I think your DIY input device idea is great!  

You're right to be cautious about using it in reaction time studies though.  I don't think the 3 meters of wire connecting the footpedal to the mouse circuit board will significantly affect reaction times. The capacitance of a wire that long only impacts signals in the multi-kilohertz range, which is far beyond the range of reaction times.

The biggest source of error is likely to be the USB mouse itself. To determine this, I recommend building a test jig that simulates switch closures at a known frequency. Then, use DMDX's test modes to measure the variability. 


Best,
Jonathan

subject: RE: [saml-dev] NameID-less SAML Subject
body:
I believe the consensus is that bearer SubjectConfirmation doesn't necessitate a NameID. 

Generally, I don't think any subject confirmation method requires one. Confirmation typically involves someone directly presenting an assertion along with proof of legitimacy. In the case of bearer, this might involve the time of presentation or a client address.

In this model, the identity of the presenter is implicit, and the assertion data is understood to be about that implicit identity. The NameID is simply one way to convey this type of data, alongside attributes.

We primarily utilize NameID in subsequent message exchanges, such as SingleLogout.

subject: PAPER 4
body:
Viceroy: A Scalable and Dynamic Emulation of the Butterfly, by Dahlia Malkhi, Moni Naor, and David Ratajczak, addresses the challenge of extreme-scale networks.  

The paper focuses on achieving high service robustness in the face of node joins and exits, as well as mitigating bottlenecks caused by high search throughput, leading to reduced network congestion.  While previous ring-based structures have successfully tackled these issues, Viceroy introduces a novel approach inspired by CAN. Instead of relying on logarithmic overhead for caching shortcuts around the ring, Viceroy utilizes O(1) pointers, enabling infinite scalability with only local updates for joins and exits.

This approach potentially sacrifices path length to resources, but the paper argues that performance remains acceptable in this dimension with high probability. Each node, in addition to its nodeID, possesses a level ID, allowing it to maintain seven pointers: next and previous pointers within the nodeID space [0,1), pointers to neighboring nodes at the same level, and pointers to nodes at levels one greater and one less, located nearby.

Routing in Viceroy involves directing messages to a level 1 node, which takes approximately log(N) steps due to the logarithmic number of levels.  Subsequently, messages traverse back down using 1

subject: starship-design: Nanotech Might Cut Solarcell Costs
body:
University of California, Berkeley chemists have discovered a method to create inexpensive plastic solar cells that are flexible enough to be painted onto any surface. These solar cells have the potential to power wearable electronics or other low-power devices.

While their initial solar cells achieve an efficiency of 1.7 percent, significantly lower than the 10 percent efficiency of current commercial photovoltaics, the researchers believe this technology holds immense promise. "Our efficiency is not good enough yet by about a factor of 10, but this technology has the potential to do a lot better," said A. Paul Alivisatos, professor of chemistry at UC Berkeley and a member of the Materials Science Division of Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory. "There is a pretty clear path for us to take to make this perform much better."

The team's development, reported in the March 29 issue of Science, involves a hybrid solar cell structure composed of tiny nanorods dispersed in an organic polymer or plastic. This 200-nanometer-thick layer, sandwiched between electrodes, can currently produce about 0.7 volts. The electrode layers and nanorod/polymer layers can be applied in separate coats, simplifying production. Unlike traditional semiconductor-based photovoltaic devices, these plastic solar

You mentioned installing a GeForce FX 5500 128MB DDR card to see if the integrated system was the issue, but it didn't resolve the problem. I understand you're still looking for a solution.

Have you checked your machine for malware and viruses?  Have you tried temporarily disabling your antivirus software if you have one?  Uninstalling any recently installed unusual software might also be helpful. It seems something might be interfering with your machine's ability to render bitmaps, causing them to display as black.

If you have an extra hard drive, consider performing a clean install of XP to see if that eliminates the problem.  If your onboard video is still enabled but not in use, disabling it in the BIOS could potentially make a difference.  Additionally, if you're using an 8-bit (256 color) video mode, try switching to a 16 or 32-bit mode. While DMDX typically promotes bitmaps to the display's color depth, many 8-bit video modes are outdated and may cause issues.

subject: Re: speed of the board
body:
Subject: Re: speed of the board

Hi Anton,

I'm concerned about the 2MHz clock speed of the HB.  The robot I'm building has a mouse at its base, and I'm writing the program to read the digital pulses from it and calculate the position relative to the starting point. This involves numerous square root and unit vector additions.  

Will you need to use trigonometric functions as well? I've developed software that functions as a 2-D odometer, utilizing encoders on the two wheels. It required me to write sin, cos, and atan2 functions.

I'm curious about how you're obtaining rotational information from the mouse. Does your robot not turn, or is the mouse positioned off-center from the wheels' centerline, allowing you to calculate rotation from the x-axis and use the y-axis for forward/backward motion?

Given the limited processing power, I suspect you might need to write a significant amount of assembly code or consider using a different language than IC. I ended up creating an integer-based trigonometric library that enabled me to perform sin and cos calculations within an interrupt function. However, I was using a faster processor and had ample memory for table lookups.

subject: [Baseline] Raising chickens the high-tech way
body:
Subject: [Baseline] Raising chickens the high-tech way

I recently came across two articles that really caught my attention: "From Egg to Drummies: 10 Weeks From Farm to Food" by Larry Barrett and "And On the 47th Day, This Chicken Went to Market" by Debbie Gage, both from Baseline Magazine.

The first article, "From Egg to Drummies," paints a rather stark picture of a chicken's journey from farm to plate, highlighting the various parts of the bird being used for different purposes. It made me realize just how much of a chicken's life is controlled and processed.

The second article, "And On the 47th Day," delved into the surprising amount of computer technology used in raising livestock. I was particularly struck by how quickly chickens are raised these days. It made me wonder how long it will be before genetic engineering is used to create livestock that are even more efficient for industrial farming, potentially leading to chickens that are dumber, meatier, and better suited to confined environments.


- Jim

subject: [UAI] CFP: Workshop on belief revision, belief merging and social choice
body:
This workshop, part of the Augustus De Morgan series, will focus on the interconnected fields of belief revision, belief merging, and social choice theory. 

Taking place from November 8th to 10th at King's College London, the event aims to foster collaboration between researchers from these disciplines and encourage the exchange of ideas.  

These three areas share fundamental principles. Belief revision explores how agents modify their beliefs when confronted with new information that conflicts with existing ones. This process often involves retracting some old beliefs to accommodate the new.  Belief merging extends this concept to the aggregation of individual belief bases into a collective one.  Social choice theory, on the other hand, deals with aggregating individual preferences to determine a collectively preferred outcome.  

Despite these clear connections, the exploration of their relationships is relatively new. The workshop will feature presentations by leading experts in belief revision, belief merging, and social choice theory, as well as opportunities for submissions of original papers and extended abstracts on related topics.

Areas of interest include:

* Belief revision
* Belief merging
* Social choice theory
* Decision making
* Preference handling
* Aggregation of preferences and judgments
* Negotiations in multi-agent systems
* Theories of order
* Fuzzy preferences
* Voting

body:
This email thread discusses various topics related to SquirrelMail, Perl, and DKIM.

**SquirrelMail Migration:**

Marcio Toledo inquired about migrating data from Openwebmail to SquirrelMail. Ken A. explained that Openwebmail uses mbox files, which can be directly imported into SquirrelMail if the server uses mbox storage. For maildir storage, conversion tools are available. Address books need to be converted to SquirrelMail's format, which is a simple text file. SquirrelMail has an import plugin for address books in CSV format. Calendars can also be migrated similarly.

**Perl Variable in Command Line:**

Yoyoyo Yoyoyoyo asked about using Perl variables in command line executions. Yitzle provided a code example demonstrating how to capture the output from backticks and use it within a Perl script.

subject: re: polynomial variability
body:
Dear Prof. Kearfott, Demmel, and Jiawang Nie,

Thank you for your prompt response.

I apologize if my initial description was unclear. My goal isn't to precisely determine the range of the polynomial, but rather to identify the region where the function exhibits the least variability.  

Essentially, I need to find the interval where the overestimation of the function's extension is minimized. It's sufficient to know that the overestimation is lowest within this interval compared to overestimations obtained for intervals of the same width, but centered elsewhere.

This region of minimum variability may not necessarily coincide with a minimum or maximum of the function f(x).  If we define the amplitude of the function's extension as w(f(X(k))) = max(f(X(k))) - min(f(X(k))), we are seeking the X(k) that corresponds to the minimum w(f(X(k))).

> Do you mean the local branches? If yes, then 'rm -rf mymergedbranch' does exactly what you want. :)

Notice that, unlike subversion, this command will permanently delete the entire branch (unless you had previously pushed it elsewhere or branched it from another location).

If this is a shared repository, the actual revisions will still be stored, but inaccessible. This is because the branch files contain information about the head revision.

subject: [DMDX] DMDX 3.1.0.0
body:
Today marks a significant milestone for DMDX with the introduction of 2D input support, hence the substantial version number update.

A new keyword, <2DInputDevice text1 text2,N1,N2,N3,N4 [...]>, allows you to designate a 2D input device, such as a touchscreen.  

* **text1:**  Must be the name of an existing, calibrated DirectInput device (unless it's "mouse", see below).  Once selected, this device cannot be deselected within the current item file without starting a new one using the chain keyword. You can, however, unmap all device buttons using the "." command. Names containing spaces require quotation marks.

* **text2:**  Provides an arbitrary name for a screen region that will trigger a response (with added "+" and "-" symbols for button presses and releases).

* **N1, N2, N3, N4:** Define the region's coordinates. N1 is the leftmost coordinate, N2 is the topmost, N3 is one more than the rightmost, and N4 is one more than the bottom (similar to a standard Windows RECT structure).  Repeat text2,N1,N2,

subject: Radio 5CX News: Message of importance.
body:
This message is a collection of bizarre and seemingly unconnected statements.  

For example, we see a skyscraper giving a pink slip to a garbage can, a deficit inside a particle accelerator learning a lesson from cough syrup, and a dolphin hesitating about a wedge while a deficit inside a garbage can writes a love letter to a ball bearing sommelier.  

These sentences are juxtaposed with others that seem equally random, creating a surreal and nonsensical narrative. 

We also see recurring themes of objects interacting in unexpected ways, such as a vacuum cleaner dancing with a recliner, a power drill eating a buzzard, and a salad dressing finding lice on a sandwich. 

The overall effect is one of playful absurdity, challenging the reader to find meaning in the seemingly meaningless.

subject: Re: sfio vs bio
body:
While sfio offers more comprehensive features, such as customizable error handlers for streams and integration with operating system functionalities like memory-mapped files, it's important to consider the scale of each library.  Sfio comprises approximately 7,000 lines of code (or 10,000 if you include stdio compatibility), significantly larger than bio's 700 lines.  

It's crucial to remember that size doesn't directly translate to performance or utility. Comparing sfio and bio is akin to comparing apples and kumquats – they serve different purposes and possess distinct strengths.

subject: PAPER 8
body:
This email discusses three papers focusing on security vulnerabilities and defense mechanisms in peer-to-peer (P2P) networks.

The first paper, "The Sybil Attack" by John R Douceur, explores the inherent risk of redundancy in P2P systems. While redundancy can mitigate failures, malicious entities with sufficient resources can exploit it to gain significant control. The paper argues that centralized authorities can prevent Sybil attacks, but decentralized systems require unrealistic assumptions about attacker capabilities.  A key challenge is ensuring distinct identities correspond to distinct entities, as a single attacker controlling multiple identities can compromise data integrity and lead to leakage. The paper proposes a method for verifying entity separation through computationally intensive tasks, but acknowledges its limitations in preventing collusion and its reliance on a somewhat weak assumption about resource distribution.

The second paper, "Defending Against Eclipse Attacks on Overlay Networks" by Atul Singh, Miguel Castro, Peter Druschel, and Antony Rowstron, addresses the threat of Eclipse attacks, where attackers compromise a large portion of a node's neighbors, effectively isolating it. This attack can be launched through Sybil attacks or collusion and manipulates the overlay maintenance algorithm. The paper suggests bounding the indegree and outdegree of nodes to mitigate this attack.  A proposed defense mechanism

subject: Virgin's latest airliner.
body:
Virgin's latest airliner, the 130 million pound Airbus 340-600 named Claudia Nine after model Claudia Schiffer, has encountered an unexpected issue.  

The plane, which entered service just weeks ago, features a 5ft x 4ft mother and baby room equipped with a plastic changing table. However, this seemingly innocent amenity has become a target for passengers seeking to join the Mile High Club.  

Virgin has been forced to replace the table multiple times due to its misuse.  Initially, German engineers at Airbus were perplexed by the repeated damage.  They couldn't fathom why the table, designed to withstand the weight of a mother and child, was being broken.  One worker humorously remarked, "We couldn't work it out. Then the penny dropped. body:
Adam,

You mentioned that product bundling works, which is an interesting point.

You also said that this arrangement is similar to "normal" marriage, except the woman gets a man in a country with opportunities for herself and her children, and avoids certain negative choices.  

Let me be clear: we don't live in a fantasy world where women are solely defined by their domestic roles. Women are capable of being productive, independent individuals who are not limited to cooking, cleaning, or serving as sexual objects.  

Sometimes, and this might surprise you, men and women actually fall in love and choose to marry for companionship and mutual affection.  It's a concept that might seem idealistic, but it's a reality for many people.  

If a man approached my mother 25 years ago and tried to convince her to marry him based solely on her cleaning skills, we wouldn't be having this conversation.

These situations can work if both parties are aware that it's a transactional arrangement: he wants a maid, a companion, and a sexual partner without financial obligation, while she seeks a country with economic stability and a lower risk of AIDS.  It's a contract, not a marriage based on love.

body:
Subject: Re: [SM-USERS] Cannot login through imapproxy

Hi,

I'm running uw-imapd on my Debian Etch home server, serving mailboxes through squirrelmail using an SSL connection. Following squirrelmail's documentation, I installed imapproxy from the Debian repositories and configured it to use port 143 for connecting to the server and port 144 for squirrelmail connections. I also followed the README.ssl instructions to generate the necessary keys.

However, when I attempt to authenticate, I encounter an error. The log shows the following message:

IMAP_Line_Read(): Protocol error.  Line terminated by LF, not CRLF

I'm unsure if this is a bug or a configuration issue on my end.  My Google searches haven't yielded many results, with only a single unanswered post on this mailing list and a few scattered mentions. I'm at a loss as to where to begin debugging.

Could you offer any suggestions? It's possible that the issue lies with Debian's implementation or a specific tweak I made.  Perhaps the imapproxy community would have more insight into this problem.

subject: Re: [opensuse] Reinstall KDE4.
body:
Subject: Re: [opensuse] Reinstall KDE4

Tom Cada wrote:

> I updated KDE4 to the latest version 4.01. Now, when I start it, none of the widgets (icons?) are displayed. There are just items showing a message that the object cannot be displayed. Additionally, there's no wallpaper or taskbar.

> One thought I have is that I never removed previous versions before installing the new one.

The switch from the KDE:KDE4 repository to the KDE:KDE4:STABLE:Desktop repository has initiated a new sequence of package release numbers. This means you need to update all packages from the KDE:KDE4:STABLE:Desktop repository, even if the already installed package appears newer.

> What's the best way to remove the existing installation so that a fresh install can be performed?

In Yast, you can set the filter to the KDE:KDE4:STABLE:Desktop repository. Then, you can choose to update all displayed packages, disregarding their package version and release.

Cheers,
Herbert

subject: Re: [Python-3000] Python source code on Bazaar vcs
body:
Subject: Re: [Python-3000] Python source code on Bazaar vcs

Barry Warsaw announced that the Python source code for versions 2.5, 2.6, and 3.0 is now publicly available through the Bazaar distributed version control system.

Thank you to Barry and the entire team for this great work!

Ubuntu users will need to install a newer version of bzr before they can check out the sources.  Here's how:

1. Open the `/etc/apt/sources.list.d/bzr.list` file with `sudo vi`.
2. Add the following lines:
   ```
   deb http://ppa.launchpad.net/bzr/ubuntu gutsy main
   deb-src http://ppa.launchpad.net/bzr/ubuntu gutsy main
   ```
3. Update your package lists with `sudo apt-get update`.
4. Install bzr, bzr-gtk, and bzrtools using `sudo apt-get --force-yes -y install bzr bzr-gtk bzrtools` (note the `--force-yes` flag as the packages are not yet signed).

subject: DeMorgan's Law doesn't work
body:
I'm trying to find the first filename matching the pattern "w[0-9][0-9]" that's unused in two directories. 

My current code works:

```
nn := (0 to 9) || (0 to 9),
not exists( downloadDir || "w" || nn ),
not exists( targetDir   || "w" || nn )
)|stop( "No names of the form w[0-9][0-9] were unused." )
```

However, the separation of the two "not exists" calls feels clunky, reminiscent of Algol-family languages' need for explicit disjunctions like `if importantNumber = 1 | importantNumber = 2`.  Snobol-family languages handle this more elegantly with `importantNumber = 1 | 2`.  

Ideally, Icon should allow factoring out the two "not exists" calls.  

I tried this:

```
nn := (0 to 9) || (0 to 9),
not exists( (downloadDir|targetDir) || "w" || nn )
)|stop( "No names of the form w[0-9][0-9] were unused

(In reply to comment #6)
> What's the problem here?  The amount of whitespace is irrelevant.

I disagree with this assessment. If the original headers appear as:

  Header1: value1
  Header2: value2

then `get("ALL")` should not return:

  Header1:  value1
  Header2:  value2

with extra spaces added between the header names and their values. While I understand the potential benefit of simplifying excessive whitespace to a single space, adding spaces where they don't exist in the original string is, in my opinion, a bug.

subject: A Non-Integer Power Function on the Pixel Shader
body:
From Wolfgang Engel's "ShaderX" book published by Wordware, this feature showcases a clever shader trick. It provides a per-pixel approximation of a non-integer power function, achieving good results for input values ranging from 0 to 1.

subject: Re: Truth-Table generator
body:
You mentioned wanting a more general truth table generator that could handle more than two variables (t1 and t2).  

While extending the code for a fixed number of variables is possible, it becomes increasingly complex. Here's an example:

```
procedure main()

   every (t1 := (0|1)) & (t2 := (0|1)) & (t3 := (0|1)) do {
      every writes(format( t1 | t2 | t3			# variables
                           "|" |			# separator
                           iand(t1,t2) | ior(t1,t2) |	# functions
			   ixor(t1,t2) | iand(t1, ior(t2,t3)) |
                           ior(iand(t1,t2),iand(t1,t3))
                  ))
      write()
      }

end
```

There are techniques to generalize this approach for 'n' variables using generators, but I've forgotten the specifics. I was hoping someone would take the existing solution and expand it.  

If I recall correctly, those techniques were quite intricate.

subject: opd mods
body:
Subject: opd mods

Hi Bill,

I've made some modifications to the focus command in the optical pointing daemon and wanted to share the details.  I've added two new parameters to the /etc/opdrc file:

* **BACKLASH0:** This parameter, previously known as the term used to date, has had its sign changed from -2000 to 2000 to evaluate its impact.
* **BACKLASH1:** This is the relaxation term you requested, and it should have the same sign as BACKLASH0.

You can edit the opdrc file directly on kueppers as smtobs, and the opd will automatically reread the changes without requiring a restart.

The focus command now has only two options, as shown in the following help response:

```
help focus
  focus [-a] [-m steps]
    opt a       - autofocus mode
    opt m       - manual focus mode
      steps     - number of steps to move
```

Currently, the manual focus mode utilizes the new backlash terms, while the autofocus mode does not. I'm still working on refining the autofocus mode modifications.

subject: A Living Memorial
body:
The Wall Street Journal's recent article, "A Living Memorial," raises a crucial point: the best tribute to those lost in the World Trade Center tragedy is not a static monument, but a vibrant, thriving city.  

The article rightly laments the current focus on the size and scope of the memorial, arguing that it overshadows the urgent need to rebuild downtown Manhattan and restore its economic vitality. The loss of 100,000 jobs following the attacks cannot be ignored, and the city's political and economic leaders should prioritize the return of commerce to the area.

The article criticizes the commission tasked with overseeing the site's redevelopment for prioritizing the memorial over rebuilding. It suggests a more pragmatic approach, similar to the Pentagon's strategy: rebuild first and then dedicate a smaller space for a memorial.

Furthermore, the article highlights the influence of anti-development activists who seek to transform the World Trade Center site into a vast park. This, it argues, would be a disservice to the memory of those who perished, as it would perpetuate the image of the site as a cemetery rather than a symbol of resilience and renewal.

subject: Motor LED's
body:
I'm noticing something unusual with the LED indicators on my motor ports. When I use the `motor(1, speed)` function, both LED motors light up. However, when I use `fd(1)`, only one LED illuminates.  

Is it normal for both LEDs to activate when using the `motor(1, speed)` function? Could this indicate an issue with the motor driver chips? This behavior occurs across all motor ports. 


Let me know if this is expected or if it suggests a problem.

subject: Re: fatal: bad string length 0 < 1: virtual_mailbox_base
body:
Wietse Venema suggested checking the output of `postmap -s hash:/etc/postfix/transport` to see if there were any issues with the /etc/postfix/transport file.  

The output of that command showed the following:

```
csadm.just.gov.ab.ca            smtp:[192.168.133.177]
ntsjam.just.gov.ab.ca           smtp:[192.168.133.177]
```

Despite this, mail was still being sent to the virtual delivery agent.  Wietse suggested using the following command to see if any other files might be causing the issue:

```
find /etc/postfix -type f -print | grep -v virtual | xargs strings -f | grep virtual
```

He also suggested finding the queue file and using `postsuper -r queueid` to investigate further.

This email is unrelated to the previous discussion about the `fatal: bad string length 0 < 1: virtual_mailbox_base` error.

subject: Walking Robot Question
body:
I'm curious about how servos are controlled in a walking robot.  

It seems to me that multiple legs would need to move simultaneously and in perfect coordination.  If servos were adjusted individually, one leg would likely drag the others, making walking impossible.  A control system that can manage multiple servos but only changes them one at a time doesn't appear suitable for this task.

What type of controller would be used for this kind of intricate movement?  Have I missed any crucial aspects of this process?

subject: Re: porting unix progs to plan9
body:
I'm concerned that simply porting *nix programs to Plan 9, just for the sake of familiarity, might detract from the unique strengths of Plan 9's toolset. 

In areas where Plan 9 lacks specific functionality, like a news reader, I believe it's more beneficial to develop new applications that leverage Plan 9's superior programming environment. For instance, I've started outlining a calendar management file system, and dhog is working on a news reading file system that will seamlessly integrate with other Plan 9 tools, particularly help(1).

P.S.  help(1) is fantastic. Spend an hour with it, and you'll never want to go back.  There are even rumors of an even more powerful successor called acme.

subject: re : implementing term - structure of correlations for power
body:
Tanya,

While there are seasonal correlations in power, particularly for NP-15 and SP-15 (within the same region), the term structure of correlations can be input. However, the same correlation structure with similar periodicity might not apply between NP-15 and, for example, RLB (Neepool), although we would expect a seasonal relationship (summer/winter) with increased noise.

Even if we implement a correlational term structure for power, different rules would need to be inputted for different regions.

Naveen

---

Vladimir,

Regarding the implementation of the term structure of correlations for power:

1. Correlations are periodic with a period of one year. This means we can utilize 12 correlation matrices calculated from the first 12 forward contracts and apply these matrices to other forward months.

2. Using a decay factor can make the curves smoother.

3. Implementing multiple correlation matrices will not significantly impact the speed of calculations in the VAR model.

Please provide your response.

Thanks,

Tanya

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] trunk buildbot status
body:
To help troubleshoot the buildbot issue, I suggest navigating to your trunk root directory and running Tool\buildbot\build.bat. This script will automatically download all necessary dependencies and initiate the build process using vcbuild.  Building through the command line sometimes requires more attention than using Visual Studio, which usually handles things correctly.

I recently built the trunk on a Windows XP x86 machine with only Visual C++ Express 2008 installed. While I encountered errors with sqlite, tcl, db-4.4.20, and ssl, the interpreter successfully built and appears to run without issues. However, since I don't have bsddb installed, I wasn't able to execute the specific build step that's causing problems.

I can install bsddb within the next 24 hours if needed.

subject: [UAI] Final CFP: ML for Surveillance & Event Detection @ ICML-2006
body:
**Workshop Description**

The task of monitoring routinely collected data for anomalous events is common across various domains. This inspection, often referred to as event detection, serves purposes ranging from security surveillance, where a guard analyzes video footage for intrusions, to scientific data analysis, where scientists identify anomalies leading to new discoveries.  Event detection holds immense potential for a wide range of real-world applications, spanning security, finance, public health, medicine, biology, environmental science, manufacturing, astrophysics, business, and economics.

Traditionally, humans manually examined collected data for event detection. However, the increasing volume and complexity of data, driven by advancements in computing and global interconnectivity, necessitate more efficient solutions. While simple data types like univariate time series can be effectively monitored using established techniques like regression, Box-Jenkins models, and statistical quality control, more complex data, including multivariate records, images, video footage, audio recordings, spatial and spatio-temporal data, text documents, and relational data, pose new challenges.

Existing machine learning paradigms often struggle to address the unique characteristics of event detection applications. Standard supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, and active learning methods frequently fall short due to the specific nature of these problems. Consequently, the development of novel algorithms is crucial to fill this
